title: Multisummability Relative to Certain Quasianalytic Classes
speaker: Patrick Speissegger
abstract:
Using Tougeron’s characterization of multisummable (in the positive real directions) series, the latter can be viewed as infinite series of convergent power series with radii of convergence shrinking to 0. In joint work with Jean-Philippe Rolin and Tamara Servi we showed that, if we replace “convergent power series” with “convergent generalized power series”, we obtain a larger class of multisummable (in the positive real directions) series. This class is shown to generate an o-minimal expansion RG∗
, whose expansion by the exponential function then defines the restrictions to some unbounded interval of both the Gamma and zeta functions. More recently, Ilgwon Seo has been further generalizing this construction by replacing “convergent power series” with “almost regular generalized power series”. The resulting Hardy field is a first step towards filling the remaining gap in Ilyashenko’s proof of Dulac’s problem.

proof_stages.label: Dulac reduction to transseries for transition maps
proof_stages.summary: The talk is motivated by planar analytic vector fields, where transition maps near elementary singularities lead Dulac's problem to the open task of finding a compositional group of germs containing these maps and embedding injectively into transseries.

proof_stages.label: Obstacles from complicated transseries and composition
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that compositions of transition maps can have transseries supports of order type larger than omega and that tracking asymptotics using only composition is too weak, motivating a field closed under algebraic operations, differentiation, and composition, and separating almost regular from multisummable sources.

proof_stages.label: Almost regular germs on standard quadratic domains
proof_stages.summary: Almost regular germs are defined by real exponents tending to infinity, polynomial logarithmic coefficients with positive constant leading coefficient, and bounded holomorphic continuation to a standard quadratic domain in a logarithmic chart with the corresponding asymptotic expansion.

proof_stages.label: Quasianalytic uniqueness and closure obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Ilyashenko's Phragmen-Lindelöf uniqueness principle gives injectivity of the Dulac-series map for these germs, while the special leading-coefficient condition explains closure under composition but also shows why almost regular germs are not closed under subtraction and why iterated logarithms must be admitted.

proof_stages.label: Base log-free algebra and fraction field
proof_stages.summary: The construction begins with the algebra A0 of almost regular-type germs whose coefficients are real constants, forms its fraction field K0, and embeds it injectively into generalized Laurent/power series using the uniqueness principle.

proof_stages.label: Relative coefficient-field iteration via slowing by L
proof_stages.summary: To incorporate logarithmic coefficients, K0 is slowed by composition with L(x)=-1/log x and used as a coefficient field to build A1 and K1, whose expansions allow meaningful truncations beyond the first omega block; repeating this produces fields with higher iterated logarithms.

proof_stages.label: Countable-union Hardy field theorem and multisummable frontier
proof_stages.summary: After countably many iterations one obtains a Hardy field K_AR containing all almost regular germs, closed under differentiation and composition with a composition-preserving transseries embedding, while the analogous inclusion of multisummable germs remains a hoped-for extension.

examples.name: Power-log transseries with support of type omega squared
examples.note: A double family of monomials such as powers of x times powers of log x, ordered lexicographically, makes concrete how transseries supports can have order type omega^2 and why truncation becomes subtle.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes an explicit power-log transseries, analyzes its lexicographic monomial ordering, and explains why its support has order type omega squared

examples.name: Analytic germ given by a convergent power series
examples.note: A germ represented by a convergent power series is presented as a simple member of the almost regular class, illustrating that the definition extends ordinary analytic germs while allowing nonconvergent asymptotics.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks that a convergent-power-series germ fits the almost-regular definition by using its holomorphic extension and boundedness in the logarithmic chart

examples.name: Generalized power series with exponents accumulating at one
examples.note: A series like a sum of terms x^(1-1/n), followed by further terms, illustrates that the generalized power-series target allows well-ordered supports beyond the ordinary omega type.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker gives a concrete generalized power series with exponents tending to one and discusses its convergence, accumulation, and resulting order type behavior

passes.reason: Its exponents accumulate at the finite value 1, a limiting support phenomenon, used to illustrate broader generalized series.
